description:
You are a site-reliability engineer who has just inherited a very small
    uptime-monitoring repository located in
    /home/user/monitoring.  
    Your first job is to prepare a flat list of every target
    the monitoring system must probe, based on the existing
    INI configuration file.

    1. Locate the file  
       /home/user/monitoring/monitors.ini  

       The file is already present and contains three
       INI sections, one per monitored service:
           [web]   – an external HTTPS endpoint  
           [db]    – an internal PostgreSQL socket  
           [cache] – an internal Redis socket  

    2. Parse monitors.ini and create a new plain-text file  
       /home/user/monitoring/check_targets.list  

       • The file **must** contain exactly three lines, one for
         each section, with no leading or trailing blank lines.  
       • Each line **must** follow this exact format  
           <section_name>:<url_value><NEWLINE>  
         where <section_name> is literally the section’s name
         and <url_value> is the value of its “url” key
         exactly as written in monitors.ini.  
       • Preserve the following order of sections:
         web, db, cache (top-to-bottom).  
       • The resulting file **must** therefore look *exactly*
         like this (including the final newline character on
         the third line):

             web:https://example.com
             db:tcp://db.internal:5432
             cache:tcp://cache.internal:6379

    3. Create a companion log file  
       /home/user/monitoring/parse.log  
       capturing the full STDOUT that your single terminal
       command produced while building check_targets.list.
       If your command produced no output, write the single
       line “(no output)” followed by a newline.

    Constraints:
    • Achieve the whole transformation with one shell
      command (it can contain pipes and redirects).
    • Do not modify monitors.ini.
    • Do not use sudo or root privileges.

    Once finished, you should have these two new files:

    /home/user/monitoring/check_targets.list
    /home/user/monitoring/parse.log

    After creating both files, you are done.
truth:
Initial state (already present before the agent starts):

    1. Directory: /home/user/monitoring  (mode 755, user-writable)

    2. File: /home/user/monitoring/monitors.ini (mode 644)
       Exact contents:
           [web]
           url = https://example.com
           expected_status = 200

           [db]
           url = tcp://db.internal:5432
           expected_status = alive

           [cache]
           url = tcp://cache.internal:6379
           expected_status = alive

    Verification expectations *after* successful completion:

    A. File: /home/user/monitoring/check_targets.list (mode 644)
       Exact byte-for-byte contents (with a final trailing newline):
           web:https://example.com
           db:tcp://db.internal:5432
           cache:tcp://cache.internal:6379

    B. File: /home/user/monitoring/parse.log (mode 644)
       • The file exists.  
       • It contains either:
         – the literal string “(no output)\n” if the command
           produced no STDOUT,  
         – or otherwise whatever the agent’s command printed,
           captured verbatim (including newlines).  
       • The automated test will allow either variant,
         because the command that the agent chooses is not
         predetermined.

    The grading script will:
       1. Confirm that /home/user/monitoring/check_targets.list
          exists and matches the exact 3-line payload above.
       2. Confirm that /home/user/monitoring/parse.log exists
          and is non-empty (≥1 byte).
name: task_000000_802d6026
